title: Iran Signals Houthis to Close Bab el-Mandeb, Greece Blocks EU Russian LNG Sanctions, and Senate Targets Russia Energy Buyers With 100% Tariffs
subhead: Tehran's reported order to prepare Houthi closure of the Red Sea chokepoint would create a dual-chokepoint crisis with Hormuz already at a standstill, while EU sanctions unity fractures over LNG and the US Senate escalates economic warfare on Russia's energy customers.

sections.heading: Iran Tells Houthis to Ready Bab el-Mandeb Closure
sections.body:
Three sources told Reuters on July 16 that Iran has instructed Yemen's Houthis to prepare to close the Bab el-Mandeb Strait — the southern gateway to the Red Sea — if the United States strikes Iranian infrastructure. The US began those strikes on July 17, making the threat immediately operational.

According to Forbes, IRGC personnel are already in Yemen controlling the decision on when to move, and the Houthis have positioned missiles and drones in the highlands overlooking Hodeidah and the Gulf of Aden, awaiting the order.

If executed, a Bab el-Mandeb closure would create a dual-chokepoint scenario where neither of the Middle East's two main oil export corridors — Hormuz and the Red Sea — could operate normally. This has not happened in the modern era of global shipping.

EU foreign policy chief Kaja Kallas visited Djibouti on July 17 to sign a new EU-Djibouti Status of Forces Agreement for Operation Aspides, warning that 'the situation in the Red Sea is once again deteriorating.' She noted that Iranian attacks on commercial vessels are 'unravelling the interim agreement with the United States.'

Bab el-Mandeb carries 10-15% of global maritime trade and, critically, has become the main bypass route for Gulf exporters trying to avoid Hormuz. Kpler data shows total petroleum volumes through the strait reached 7.4 million barrels per day in June — up from 4.2 million bpd a year earlier — as Saudi Arabia redirected over 70% of its crude exports to the Red Sea port of Yanbu. A closure now would trap the very traffic that was diverted to escape Hormuz.

The Philippines flagged that it is among the most vulnerable nations to fuel shortages from a Bab el-Mandeb shutdown, while India's think-tank community noted that roughly half of India's merchandise exports transit the Red Sea corridor.
sections.whyItMatters: A dual closure of Hormuz and Bab el-Mandeb would be unprecedented, eliminating the Middle East's two main export routes simultaneously and forcing all remaining Gulf oil and LNG onto Cape of Good Hope routes — adding 2-3 weeks and massive cost to every shipment.

sections.heading: Hormuz Traffic Collapses to Three Vessels as Strikes Continue
sections.body:
Just three commodity vessels transited the Strait of Hormuz on Thursday, Reuters reported — the fewest daily transits since May, with most ships halting or making U-turns after recent Iranian attacks on shipping. A maritime risk CEO described conditions as a 'worst-case scenario' for oil tankers.

One seafarer was killed and three injured in an attack on the crude oil tanker Al Bahyah off Oman's coast on Tuesday, according to the report. The US military has renewed strikes on Iran while simultaneously enforcing a naval blockade of all Iranian ports, as Trump re-imposed the blockade on July 14.

Trump also proposed charging a 20% fee to guard the Strait of Hormuz, Reuters reported, adding a commercial dimension to the military confrontation. Oil prices were on track for a weekly gain exceeding 10%, with August WTI crude closing up $3.54 (4.48%) on Friday. Asian LNG prices jumped approximately 21%, according to Anadolu Agency.

The IMF warned that the global economy has 'less capacity to cushion the blow' from reduced energy supplies through Hormuz, and Chevron announced it is exploring creating a Strait of Hormuz alternative pipeline for Iraqi oil — a signal that major producers see no near-term return to normal.
sections.whyItMatters: Hormuz is functionally closed with only 3 daily transits. The market impact is compounding: oil prices up 10%+ in a week, LNG up 21%, and the IMF flagging diminishing global buffers. Each additional day of near-zero traffic tightens the physical supply squeeze.

sections.heading: Greece Blocks EU Russian LNG Sanctions to Protect Shipping Interests
sections.body:
Greece has emerged as the most vocal opponent of the EU's 21st sanctions package against Russia, blocking consensus on a ban on transferring Russian LNG to third countries. Alongside Austria and others, Athens objected during talks on Wednesday, leading EU envoys to postpone further discussions until July 23.

Greek government officials warned that the ban would 'cede market share to non-EU competitors' and argued that 'sanctions should erode Russia's economic capacity — not create strategic windfalls for others at Europe's expense,' according to Reuters.

The stakes are specific: Greece dominates Europe's LNG carrier shipping market and ranks among the world's top players. Greek shipping magnate George Procopiou's Dynagas was cited as a key interest behind the veto, TradeWinds reported. The price cap on Russian oil remains unchanged at $44.10 per barrel in the interim.

Forbes reported that EU nations purchased a record volume of LNG from Russia's Yamal terminal recently, a transaction pattern that directly undermines the strategic rationale of the sanctions regime Brussels has championed since 2022. Forbes analysis estimated cumulative European economic damage from sanctions at between €500 billion and €1.6 trillion over 2022-2025, with the US Congressional Research Service estimating up to 7% of Europe's GDP spent shielding consumers from the fallout.
sections.whyItMatters: The EU's sanctions architecture on Russia is fracturing from within. Greece's veto protects a national shipping champion while the bloc continues buying record volumes of Russian LNG — exposing the gap between declared policy and actual practice that weakens Europe's credibility and cohesion.

sections.heading: Senate Introduces Graham Russia Sanctions Bill Targeting Top Energy Buyers
sections.body:
A bipartisan group of more than 60 US senators introduced the 'Senator Lindsey O. Graham Sanctioning Russia Act of 2026' on Thursday, replacing an earlier proposal for blanket 500% tariffs with targeted tariffs of up to 100% on the world's top five purchasers of Russian crude oil and natural gas.

The bill specifically names China, India, Slovakia, Hungary, and Azerbaijan as targets, while exempting European countries whose Russian gas imports account for less than 15% of Russia's total gas exports and are taking steps to reduce them. It also exempts US purchases of Russian uranium for nuclear reactors.

The legislation incorporates the bipartisan SHADOW Fleet Sanctions Act targeting Russia's sanctions-evasion fleet and the STOP Russia-China Act targeting Chinese support for Russia's defense industrial base. It mandates sanctions on Yamal LNG, Arctic LNG 1, 2, and 3, and future Russian Arctic energy projects, along with their controlling owners and executives.

Senator Richard Blumenthal said Graham had secured White House agreement on the revised text before his death, calling from Kyiv with news that 'President Trump would support this bill.' Two House Democrats have already voiced opposition, calling the measure 'a backdoor attempt to greenlight tariffs of up to 100% on our major trading partners.'
sections.whyItMatters: If enacted, this would be the first time Congress explicitly authorizes tariffs as a geopolitical weapon to punish countries financing another nation's war. The 100% tariff threat on India and China — two of the largest economies — marks a significant escalation in secondary sanctions, though the presidential waiver and uranium exemption already reveal the fault lines.

sections.heading: IEA Critical Minerals Outlook: Supply Chains Growing More Concentrated, Not Less
sections.body:
The IEA's 2026 Global Critical Minerals Outlook, released July 17, identifies a widening range of vulnerabilities in critical mineral supply chains despite increased policy attention. Key findings:

Investment fell 9% in 2025, ending years of growth, as price volatility and geopolitical tensions discouraged capital deployment. The geographic concentration of mineral supply chains has increased further, particularly for refining — over the past two years, the top refiners accounted for more than three-quarters of total growth in refined supply.

China's rare earth export controls, introduced in April 2025 and expanded in October 2025, forced some automakers to reduce or suspend production. The IEA estimates that if fully implemented, the measures could jeopardize $6.5 trillion in annual downstream production outside China. However, new rare earth projects in the US and production increases in Malaysia have reduced China's share of refining from over 90% in 2023 to 85% in 2025, projected to fall to 70% by 2035 if planned projects come online.

Meanwhile, the Trump administration announced steps to 'secure critical minerals supply chains,' though specifics remain limited. Malaysia's parliament is seeking clarity over a Lynas-Pentagon rare earths deal, and India's Geological Survey has identified promising rare earth deposits in the country's northeast.
sections.whyItMatters: The IEA's data shows that despite years of diversification talk, critical mineral supply chains are concentrating further, not spreading out. Investment is falling while concentration is rising — the opposite of what energy transition security requires. China's $6.5T leverage tool remains largely intact.

sections.heading: Oil Markets Face Compounding Squeeze as China's Demand Cushion Fades
sections.body:
Oil prices surged more than 10% for the week as the dual Hormuz-Red Sea threat compounded an already tight physical market. August WTI closed Friday at $82.54, up 4.48% on the day, with analysts warning prices could head above $100 per barrel if both chokepoints close simultaneously.

The market's remaining cushion is thinning from multiple directions. US commercial crude stockpiles fell by 1.7 million barrels last week, exceeding analyst expectations of a 900,000-barrel draw. Diesel supplies are at dangerously low levels, with analysts warning that 'refining capacity is strained and fuel markets don't have much of a buffer.'

China's oil imports have plunged during the Iran war — Reuters reports an average of 11.5 million bpd over five years — and some market watchers now see signs the buying pause may be ending, which would remove a key cushion just as Gulf supply shrinks. BayStreet notes that if China returns to the market as a buyer while Hormuz remains constrained, the supply-demand balance could tighten dramatically.

TotalEnergies said it expects a second-quarter earnings boost from the war-driven price rally, while also acknowledging that LNG income would fall sharply. BP reported upstream production declining to 2.17-2.22 million boed in Q2, down from 2.34 million in Q1.
sections.whyItMatters: The oil market's safety margins are eroding from all sides: low inventories, strained refining, a potential return of Chinese buying, and the specter of two closed chokepoints. Any single additional disruption could push prices past $100.

bottomLine: July 18 marks the day the Middle East's dual-chokepoint threat became operational: Iran's order for Houthis to ready Bab el-Mandeb closure coincided with the US beginning infrastructure strikes, while Hormuz traffic fell to just three vessels. With Gulf exporters having already diverted 70% of Saudi crude through the Red Sea to escape Hormuz, a second closure would trap the escape route itself. Simultaneously, the Western sanctions architecture is fracturing — Greece is blocking EU Russian LNG sanctions to protect its shipping fleet, even as the US Senate escalates with 100% tariff threats against Russia's top energy buyers. The IEA's latest data confirms that critical mineral supply chains are concentrating further, not diversifying, and oil markets have lost their demand cushion just as physical supply tightens. The convergence of military escalation, sanctions disunity, and thinning market buffers means the global energy system is operating with less room for error than at any point since the war began.
sources: Reuters, Forbes, Anadolu Agency, Morningstar/Dow Jones, Firstpost, Greek City Times, Hindu Business Line, WebIndia123, IEA Global Critical Minerals Outlook 2026, NPR, TradeWinds, BayStreet, Euronews, Yahoo News
